title: Economic policy under state socialism, 1945–1989
abstract: This chapter demonstrates how ideology interacted with economic and geopolitical conditions in shaping the economic institutions and economic policy between the end of the war and the fall of communism. It describes the classical model of the command economy, which was implemented first in the Soviet Union by Joseph Stalin during the 1930s and remained in place until the death of the dictator. The chapter explores the contribution of economic policy to the collapse of state socialism in the late 1980s, and asks to what extent political factors rather than the intrinsic inefficiency of the socialist system determined its fate. From the early 1950s, Yugoslavia began to embark on building a unique version of socialism that for many developing countries represented a ‘third-way’ alternative to the Western capitalist and the Soviet communist models. Economic development in socialist countries was slowing down for at least a decade before the demise of communism in the late 1980s.
summary_en: How did ideology interact with economic and geopolitical conditions to shape state-socialist economic institutions, 1945-1989? Markevich and Vonyó describe the classical command-economy model — implemented first by Stalin in the 1930s and dominant until his death — and its variations across the Eastern Bloc. The chapter explores economic policy's contribution to the late-1980s collapse of state socialism and asks whether political factors or the intrinsic inefficiency of socialism determined its fate. Yugoslavia is highlighted as a unique 'third-way' alternative for many developing countries. A key documented pattern: economic development in socialist countries was already slowing for at least a decade before communism's demise in the late 1980s.
data_used: Historical synthesis chapter. Reviews economic-policy and growth data for Soviet Union, Yugoslavia, and Eastern Bloc countries 1945-1989, drawing on multiple historical-economic-statistics projects.
policy_relevance: Foundational reference for Hungarian transition-narrative debates. The paper's distinction between intrinsic-inefficiency and political-factor explanations for socialist collapse matters for how Hungarians interpret the 1989 transition and its sequel. Useful for KRTK and academic researchers on transition economics, and indirectly for current Hungarian institutional-economics policy debates.
